title: Effects of adaptation to altered color statistics provide evidence for calibration of color perception to the color statistics of natural scenes.

title: Visual working memory for real-world objects is resistant to visual but vulnerable to semantic interference.

llm_reason: The paper directly examines individual-level risk preferences and how the representation of choice options (goods vs. monetary credit) affects risky decision-making, which relates to mental representation, utility evaluation, and gain-loss framing under uncertainty.

title: Reference points, risk-taking behavior, and competitive outcomes in sequential settings
llm_reason: While the paper involves risk-taking behavior, it primarily analyzes field data from competitive sports to study population-level and group-level outcomes (gender, experience, rivalry) without modeling individual cognitive processes or mental representations underlying decisions.

title: Uncertainty (ambiguity) aversion and seeking
llm_reason: Ambiguity aversion and seeking are core topics in individual-level decision making under uncertainty, directly addressing how people evaluate and respond to unknown probabilities — a central concern of this research agenda.

title: Learning from the storm: The impact of hurricane experience on future disaster preparedness
llm_reason: The paper focuses on household-level behavioral outcomes in field data (consumer purchases) without modeling individual cognitive processes such as risk perception, probability weighting, or decision mechanisms. It lacks a cognitive or process-level account of how past experience shapes mental representations of risk.

title: Does signed prediction error drive declarative memory? Evidence from variable choice paradigms
llm_reason: The paper focuses on prediction error and declarative memory (word-translation learning), not on decision making under risk, uncertainty, or choice processes. While it uses a reward paradigm, the central question is about memory encoding, not how individuals evaluate or compare choice options.

title: Different sources of the numerical comparison size effect
llm_reason: The paper uses diffusion model analysis to decompose decision process parameters (e.g., decision threshold adjustment) underlying numerical comparison effects, directly addressing process-level cognitive mechanisms in a choice/comparison task under varying complexity and stimulus statistics.

title: Spotlight on the past: Focusing attention on long-term memory
llm_reason: This paper focuses on memory retrieval mechanisms and attentional processes in short-term and long-term memory paradigms, not on decision making under risk, uncertainty, or choice complexity. It does not address any aspect of the PhD student's research agenda on evaluation and comparison of choice options.
